summary: Obfuscated WhatsApp bot package self-updates from personal GitHub repo on mutable branch and deletes installer lockfiles
details: On require, index.js compares the local package version against the npm registry and, on mismatch, fetches https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ruhend2001/maleficent/main/utils-mf-package/utils-mf-<version>.tgz, extracts it to./tmp/upgrade, and recursively copies the contents over./node_modules/utils-mf. The source is a personal GitHub account on a mutable `main` branch, not a verified publisher release, and the fetched tarball is neither hash- nor signature-verified — every require() can pull fresh attacker-controlled code into the installer's node_modules. The same load path also deletes./package-lock.json and./yarn.lock from the consumer's working directory, removing the dependency-pinning records that would otherwise make installs reproducible. The advertised `main` is a single 15.7 MB obfuscator.io-output file (shuffled string-array dispatcher, _0xNNNN identifiers) actively concealing this code path. Package metadata describes the package as a generic 'utility mf', but the actual contents are a Baileys-based WhatsApp bot framework (makeWASocket, useMultiFileAuthState, plugin loader, session persistence), so any consumer importing this on the assumption of a small helper library boots an unrelated bot with file/network side effects. The combination — obfuscated body + mutable-branch self-updater from a non-publisher source + lockfile deletion + purpose misrepresentation — is an unambiguous supply-chain RCE channel.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
